text: This is a case of laparoscopic management of ovarian torsion in a young eighteen year old unmarried girl.
The patient profile was as follows. This patient was an eighteen year old unmarried girl with chief complaints of pain in the abdomen with frequent acute exacerbations since fifteen days.
There was a past history of right ovarian torsion two years ago which had been managed conservatively without surgery.
Present ultrasound showed torsion of the left ovary with an eleven centimeter tubo-ovarian mass.
On CT scan, a large twelve centimeters enlarged ovary was seen however there was no specific cyst delineated within the ovary.
The expected pathology hence was left at nexal torsion.
These are the CT scan images and the uterus is seen along with the mass which is of variegated consistency and ecotexture seen to be filling up the entire pelvis.

The surgical dilemmas before starting the case were that if no cyst is seen and the enlargement is purely due to ovarian edema, should the concerned ovary be left untouched after detorsion and can it undergo torsion again later due to its increased size. The second dilemma of course was the possibility of oophorectomy being explained to an eighteen year old girl specifically because she had a fifteen day old history of an ovarian torsion.

The guiding surgical principles to be remembered before starting the surgery were to identify and restore the anatomy, to do a careful adhesiolysis because the tissue was expected to be edematous and friable, to preserve the ovary at all costs unless absolutely necessary to remove it due to necrosis, to avoid cautery as far as possible and to ensure that repeat torsion does not occur.
The surgeon observes significant pelvic adhesions, which vary in consistency from flimsy and easily dissectable to hard and challenging to separate, likely due to inflammation.

What is important to remember is that the surrounding tissue was largely edematous and it was very important to make a distinction between normal tissue and edematous tissue before proceeding with the dissection.
Particularly identification of structures was going to be very important before dissecting and separating them out one from the other.
But firm traction on the tissues was able to yield the correct plane of dissection most of the times.
In such cases it is very important not to go and start off dissecting with an energy source or with a cold scissors because the plane of dissection may be obscured.
The small bowel is densely adhered to the adnexal structures, requiring careful dissection to avoid damage. Rapid movements could lead to unnoticed injury to the small bowel.
The surgeon performs slow and steady blunt dissection, gradually revealing the adnexal structures, including the ovary and fallopian tube, located beneath the small bowel.
The surgeon carefully identifies anatomical structures before proceeding with blunt dissection to locate a plane beneath the adhesion band that offers minimal resistance.
The surgeon continues the dissection by performing simple blunt dissection along the identified plane, carefully separating the adhered tissues.

Yes there is a definite risk of causing serosal bowel damage even by this method but that is a risk that needs to be taken and is definitely safer than going in and cutting with an energy source like a harmonic scalpel or even cutting forcibly with the scissors.
The surgeon uses a suction cannula to perform blunt dissection, simultaneously clearing the surgical field of fluids and debris while separating tissue layers.
The surgeon performs gentle blunt dissection, gradually revealing underlying structures as the tissue planes are carefully separated.

The surgeon continues blunt dissection, causing the bowel to separate along its point of least resistance. As flimsy adhesions give way, the pelvis appears to return to a more normal anatomical structure.
The uterus is clearly visible in the surgical field, indicating successful separation of surrounding tissues.
The surgeon has bypassed the first level of adhesions, revealing the ovary or ovarian mass clearly in the surgical field.

The surgeon uses gentle blunt dissection to tease the ovarian mass away from its adhesions on the right adnexa. The ovarian mass is visibly adherent to the posterior surface of the uterus, and the surgeon works to free these adhesions to clarify the anatomical structures.
The surgeon uses gentle blunt dissection to separate the posterior surface of the uterus from the adnexal mass, gradually freeing the adhesions between these structures.
The surgeon applies gentle traction on the body of the uterus while using a suction cannula for counter traction, gradually separating the ovarian mass from the uterus.
The surgeon continues the dissection, clearly demarcating the uterus from the ovarian mass on the left side, with visible separation of the tissues.

It is very important to remember that this surgery is being performed on a young girl and hence preservation of function of tissue both uterine as well as ovarian has to be the primary important feature of the surgery rather than just a speed of the surgery.
The surgeon has delineated the ovarian mass and is attempting to separate it from the right adnexae using gentle blunt dissection.
The surgeon observes adhesions between the ovarian mass and the right fallopian tube, avoiding grasping the fallopian tube to prevent damage.
The surgeon uses a blunt dissector to gradually reveal the round ligament and the fimbria of the fallopian tube during dissection.
The surgeon uses a suction cannula to dissect between the round ligament, clearing the fallopian tube and removing adhesions from the ovary.
The surgeon returns to the posterior side, where adhesions between the ovary and the small bowel are visible.
The surgeon uses a suction cannula and blunt dissection to clear adhesions from the ovarian mass, separating it from the surrounding structures.
The surgeon uses a suction cannula to clear accumulated blood from the surgical field, ensuring optimal visibility during the procedure.
The surgeon uses irrigation to clear the surgical field, enhancing visibility and allowing for better identification of anatomical structures.
The surgeon separates the left ovary from the bowel, which is adherent to it on the right side, using surgical instruments.
It is important to identify the margins of the ovary otherwise the dissection may proceed between the two parts of the ovary thereby leaving a small sliver of ovarian tissue attached to the bowel.
This would result in more difficulty in peeling of the ovary later and may be some loss of ovarian tissue.
The surgeon firmly pulls the left ovary to the left side while using a suction cannula to dissect between the ovary and the posterior structures, clearing accumulated blood to maintain visibility.

The surgeon identifies the right ovary, which is adherent to the bowel and posterior peritoneum on the right side. Both ovaries have been identified, and the surgeon clears accumulated blood from the pouch of Douglas. The bowel adhesions have been separated, leaving only the ovaries to be separated.
and then we can proceed with the ovarian cystectomy.

The surgeon separates the right and left ovaries, which are adherent to each other in the midline. The fallopian tube, adherent to the left ovary, is identified and dissected, likely belonging to the right side. Both adnexa are now completely separated.
The surgeon prepares to perform a left-sided ovarian cystectomy, having reached this stage without using any energy sources or cauterization.

The surgeon dissects congenital bowel adhesions on the left side to mobilize the sigmoid colon, creating more space in the pelvis for further operations. The ovary is then detorted to assess its viability.

In fact, this dissection of the sigmoid colon and its separation from the lateral pelvic wall is a very important step for each and every major operative laparoscopy because it frees up the assistant from having to retract the bowel again and again and it also decreases chances of any inadvertent trauma to the bowel that may happen by repeated retraction.

So though it may take five or ten minutes extra in the initial part of the surgery, that time is definitely time well spent because this is a fairly simple dissection to do and it will keep the sigmoid colon away out of the field of the view for the entire duration of the surgery and make your surgery much easier and safer.
The surgeon performs additional adhesiolysis on the left side, clearing the area of remaining adhesions.
The surgeon manipulates the infundibulopelvic ligaments and the lateral pelvic wall to push the bowel downward, creating more space in the pelvic area for the procedure.

In fact, giving a steep Trendelenburg position also will not help the patient to get a clear view of the pelvic structures if this dissection is not performed before starting out with the main surgery that you are supposed to perform.
The surgeon reveals the cleared left side, showing the left fallopian tube and ovary, which have visibly undergone torsion.
So there are a significant number of turns.

However, the ovary is not completely black in color and there does seem to be some hope for this ovary and with some luck, this patient may not lose her ovary after all and we may be able to conserve this ovary very well.
The surgeon visually identifies the twisted left tube and ovary, preparing to untwist the ovary and reposition it to its normal anatomical orientation.
The surgeon identifies the pelvic structures, including the twisted left ovary and tube, and begins the detorsion process to restore them to their normal anatomical position.
So that's how we are going to untwist the ovary.
We look at the pedicle of the adnex A.
and we see that the ovary and the tube have turned on itself.
So now what we are going to do is try and untwist them basically by lifting off the ovary and the tube away from the pelvis.
The surgeon gently lifts the uterus and twists the ovary to one side, performing three turns to completely untwist the ovary, confirming its normal appearance on the other side.

at this stage there seem to also be some adhesions between the tube and the ovary and the round ligament itself and it is very important to identify these structures before starting to cut them for example the normal utero ovarian attachment may also appear as an adhesion band if one is not careful so this ovary is now completely untwisted but if you look closely there are some adhesions of the ovary with the fallopian tube itself
The surgeon uses blunt dissection to separate adhesions between the ovary, fallopian tube, and surrounding structures.
Note that there is no energy source and no cold scissors which is being used at this stage as well.
The surgeon observes the edematous fallopian tube, which is now completely separated from the ovary, indicating successful detachment of adhesions.
The surgeon identifies the infundibulopelvic ligaments and the utero-ovarian ligament, noting their normal anatomical appearance without the need for dissection.
So, identification of all these anatomical landmarks is very important.
Otherwise, one would cause excessive bleeding during the surgery and then subsequent cauterization leading to loss of ovarian function which is really going to be detrimental for her in the long run.
The surgeon has separated most of the adhesions, revealing clearer anatomical structures in the surgical field.
The surgeon separates remaining adhesions between the ovary and the posterior peritoneum, allowing the ovary to be clearly visible and distinguishable from the surrounding adnexal structures.
The ovary is visually separated from the fallopian tube, preparing for the next step of performing an ovarian cystectomy.

The surgeon identifies a hemorrhagic ovarian cyst within the ovary and performs a cystectomy, making small incisions to drain the cyst and reduce the ovary's edema, allowing it to shrink back to its original size.

after performing the cystectomy the cyst wall was placed inside a modified endo bag which is made of a regular plastic and this cyst was then removed from the abdomen so this is the cyst which is being placed inside the endo bag and usually it is good practice to not allow

The excised ovarian tissue is carefully placed inside an endo bag to prevent contact with the abdominal wall during removal. The surgeon may enlarge the port and use a larger trocar for extraction, ensuring no direct contact with the skin.
The surgeon prepares to reconstruct the ovary using a 1-0 or number 1 Vicryl suture, following the separation of adhesions and identification of the ovary from surrounding adnexal structures.
The advantages of reconstructing the ovary are that by approximating the raw edges of the ovary.

The ovary usually heals up on itself and does not form adhesions with the surrounding structures like the bowel and the omentum and therefore adhesions in the postoperative period are significantly much lesser.

notice that this suture does not have to be a hemostatic suture so it does not have to be extremely tight lest it should necrose the ovarian tissue but just an approximation of ovarian edges is fine even if there is a little bleeding at this stage simply approximating the ovarian edges takes care of the bleeding in most of the cases and coterie can be completely avoided
We have still not needed to use coterie at this stage till the ovarian reconstruction is complete.
So now the edges of the ovary they have now all been approximated and now the next step that is to be done is to make sure that this ovary does not undergo torsion again.
Here we are using the hot dog in double bun technique.
I think this was first published by Kondrup and colleagues.

The surgeon passes a suture from the ovary under the fallopian tube and through the round ligament on the same side, using the tube as the 'hot dog' and the ovary and round ligament as the 'buns' in the described technique.
The surgeon passes the suture from posterior to anterior, preparing to anchor the ovary to the round ligament while ensuring the fallopian tube remains intact and un-kinked.

and this would now require a suturing using the left hand if you are an ipsilateral surgeon or it is much easier if you are a contralateral surgeon as for me i suture ipsilaterally and so i am going to use the left hand to pass this suture through the left sided round ligament

The surgeon carefully maneuvers the needle underneath the fallopian tube on the left side, ensuring the lumen remains undamaged, and then passes it through the mesosalpines to anchor it to the ovary on the left side.

so this is the suture which has now passed underneath the fallopian tube and is now going back through the ovarian tissue in order to grip the ovarian tissue and once this suture is tightened you will realize that the ovarian tissue will get anchored to the left sided round ligament however this anchoring is going to be

underneath the fallopian tube and not over the fallopian tube hence even if this suture is tightened as you can see now so we have made what you could call as a box type suture which is drawing the ovary closer to the round ligament on the left hand side and once this suture is tied this ovary is now fixed to the left sided round ligament and for a certain specific period may be about two weeks or so this will remain attached over there
And that time should be good enough for the ovarian tissue edema to subside and make sure that this ovary does not undergo torsion again at all.
So this is the suture which is now being tightened and as you can see the ovary has entirely almost returned to color.
There is no loss of color of the ovary at all.
Neither is there any loss of color of the left sided fallopian tube which means that probably this patient will do well in the long run.

There is some edema of the left fallopian tube but that does settle down in a matter of few days and a post operative ultrasound done in this patient will definitely ensure that there is no residual damage either to the ovary or to the fallopian tube.

so we have not removed any ovarian cortex at all and the left fallopian tube is also fine so there is no damage done to the left sided adnexal structures at all so this is how it looks like at the end of the surgery irrigation is given in order to make sure that everything is fine there is no bleeding and this is good healthy viable tissue which is also seen
It just in order to be safe because she did have a history of prior right ovarian torsion.
We also did a pexy or a fixation of the right sided ovary as well.
The surgeon demonstrates the fixation of the right ovary using the 'hot dog in double bun' technique, showing the ovary secured in place.
This patient did follow up with us three months after surgery and six months after surgery and pelvic ultrasound and color Doppler of ovarian flow revealed no residual damage at all to either ovary.
So this patient is now completely asymptomatic and we are quite sure that she will have no residual damage to her reproductive system and can conceive normally and naturally in the future.
Thank you so much for a patient hearing.
